preamble: The shift from the high front rounded vowel [y] to the mid front unrounded vowel [e] involves both Unrounding and Lowering. This process is common in dialects where front rounded vowels are lost or lowered while simultaneously losing their rounded quality.

examples.note: Widespread Unrounding and Lowering occurs in many northern Low German dialects.
Kentish dialect of Old English characteristically unrounded and lowered /y/ (from i-mutation of /u/) to /e/, whereas other dialects typically unrounded to /i/.

sources: Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
